skill.name: clawdirect-dev
skill.description: Guides developers through building agent-facing web apps with ATXP authentication, MCP tools, Express, SQLite, and cookie-based browser access.
skill.summary: Build ATXP-enabled web apps that agents can use through browsers and MCP tools.
skill.author: napoleond

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, environment, and execution patterns, but most are Markdown examples or code templates rather than active skill code. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found in SKILL.md. The skill still presents medium risk because its recommended cookie bootstrap pattern places authentication tokens in URLs and uses permissive defaults that developers could copy into production.

medium_findings.title: Authentication Cookie Passed Through URL Query String
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends passing an authentication cookie value in a query parameter, then setting an HTTP-only cookie and redirecting. This can expose bearer-style credentials through browser history, server logs, proxy logs, analytics, screenshots, or referrer headers before the redirect completes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly documented and implemented in the template. The exposure risk is a known property of putting bearer tokens in URLs, even when a later redirect removes the parameter.

medium_findings.title: Long-Lived Cookies Without Expiration or Revocation State
medium_findings.description: The template stores auth cookies in SQLite without an expiry column and sets browser cookies for 30 days. Copied as-is, stolen or logged cookie values can remain valid for a long period with no built-in revocation or rotation control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The schema creates only cookie value, account, and created timestamp fields, and the browser cookie lifetime is directly shown as 30 days. The absence of expiration enforcement is clear from the provided template.

medium_findings.title: Permissive CORS Default in Express Template
medium_findings.description: The Express setup calls CORS without an origin allowlist. This is convenient for development but can weaken production API boundaries if developers copy the template without narrowing allowed origins.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code example clearly enables default CORS middleware. The risk depends on deployment context and cookie credential settings, so confidence is high for the pattern but lower for exploitability.

low_findings.title: Static Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Python exec and many shell execution findings are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline command examples, and a SQLite db.exec call. No standalone script or active installer code is present in the scanned skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is SKILL.md, and the flagged locations are explanatory Markdown snippets. The line 122 exec call is SQLite schema initialization, not dynamic language execution.

low_findings.title: Environment and Network Findings Are Template Configuration
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to documentation, example domains, localhost endpoints, and the ClawDirect service. Environment variable examples configure database paths, ports, and ATXP funding destination rather than exfiltrating secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs and environment variables are visible in instructional context and no hidden outbound request is present. Some copied configuration can still affect production security, so the finding is kept as low context evidence.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Mostly Misclassified Terms
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged cryptography and base64-related text, but the cookie generator uses crypto.randomBytes. The base64 references describe thumbnail image input, not cryptographic protection or obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant code uses randomBytes for cookie generation, which is appropriate for random tokens. The base64 examples are image payload fields, so the weak-crypto interpretation is likely false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token in Query Parameter
dangerous_patterns.description: The auth bootstrap pattern moves a cookie value through the URL before setting an HTTP-only cookie. Treat this as a sensitive-token handling risk in production designs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly recommended and implemented. The main uncertainty is whether developers will deploy the example unchanged.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Paste Production Defaults
dangerous_patterns.description: The template is useful for learning, but it omits production hardening such as token expiry, revocation, origin restrictions, and HTTPS-only cookie requirements outside production mode.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The missing controls are visible from the template, but final risk depends on how a developer adapts the sample before deployment.

content.value_statement: Developers need a repeatable way to let AI agents authenticate, browse, and call MCP tools. This skill provides an ATXP, Express, SQLite, and MCP template for building agent-facing web services.
actual_capabilities: Explains how browser automation and MCP tools work together for agent-facing sites.
Provides a Node.js project setup using Express, TypeScript, SQLite, ATXP, and @longrun/turtle.
Shows how to create an MCP cookie tool that links an ATXP account to browser authentication.
Includes Express middleware examples for protected API routes and cookie validation.
Provides a template for publishing an agent skill that documents MCP tools and browser login.
Shows ClawDirect directory add, edit, and delete commands for agent-facing sites.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a runnable project generator.
The authentication examples need production hardening before deployment.
It assumes familiarity with Node.js, TypeScript, Express, MCP, and ATXP concepts.
It uses SQLite examples and does not cover multi-region or high-scale database designs.

use_cases.title: Prototype an Agent-Ready Service
use_cases.description: Use the template to create a small service where agents can authenticate and call MCP actions.

use_cases.title: Add Agent Browser Login
use_cases.description: Adapt the cookie flow when an existing web app needs authenticated browser access for AI agents.

use_cases.title: Publish a Discoverable Agent Skill
use_cases.description: Create clear agent instructions and list a completed service in the ClawDirect directory.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clawdirect-dev to explain how an agent-facing web app combines browser access, MCP tools, ATXP authentication, and SQLite cookies.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter Project Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clawdirect-dev to plan a TypeScript Express app with ATXP authentication, a cookie MCP tool, protected routes, and deployment notes.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt Auth for My App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clawdirect-dev to adapt the cookie authentication pattern for my existing Express app. Include security improvements for production.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clawdirect-dev to review my ATXP-enabled web app design for token handling, CORS, cookie settings, payments, and agent skill documentation.

output_examples.input: I need a first version of an agent-facing web service.
output_examples.output: A project outline with setup steps, core files, MCP tools, cookie authentication flow, and deployment requirements.

output_examples.input: I already have an Express app and want agents to log in.
output_examples.output: An integration plan that adds an ATXP-backed cookie tool, protected API middleware, and safer production settings.

output_examples.input: I want to publish my site for agent discovery.
output_examples.output: A checklist for writing the agent skill, documenting tools, setting authentication instructions, and submitting a ClawDirect entry.

best_practices: Treat the sample code as a starting point and harden authentication before production use.
Use short-lived tokens, revocation, HTTPS, strict cookie settings, and restricted CORS origins.
Document MCP tool costs, authentication steps, and browser login behavior clearly for agents.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy query-string bearer tokens without compensating controls and log hygiene.
Do not keep default CORS settings in production without an origin policy.
Do not publish paid MCP actions without clear pricing and authorization checks.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design an ATXP-enabled web app that AI agents can use through browsers and MCP tools.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete application automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance and templates that a developer adapts into a project.

faq.question: Which stack does it assume?
faq.answer: It assumes Node.js, TypeScript, Express, SQLite, @longrun/turtle, and ATXP packages.

faq.question: Is the cookie bootstrap pattern production-ready?
faq.answer: Not by itself. Add shorter token lifetimes, revocation, HTTPS enforcement, CORS restrictions, and careful log handling.

faq.question: Can I use it without payments?
faq.answer: Yes. The cookie tool can be free while paid MCP actions remain optional.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
